title: PAINT: Partner-Agnostic Intent-Aware Cooperative Transport with Legged Robots
abstract: 协作搬运需要机器人在维持稳定操作的同时，通过物理交互推断同伴意图。这一过程在复杂环境中尤为困难，因为交互信号难以捕捉和建模。我们提出PAINT框架——一种轻量级且高效的分层学习框架，用于实现同伴对抗型意图感知的协作式四足搬运，可直接通过本体感知反馈推断同伴意图。PAINT将意图理解与地形鲁棒步态解耦：高层策略通过意图估计器与师生训练机制推断同伴交互力矩，低层步态骨干网络则确保稳健执行。这种设计无需外部力觉传感或负载跟踪即可实现轻量化部署。大量仿真与实物实验表明，该方法在不同地形、负载及同伴条件下均能实现柔顺协作搬运。我们进一步证明，PAINT可自然地扩展至分布式多机器人搬运场景，并通过更换底层步态骨干网络实现跨机器人形态迁移。实验结果表明，负载耦合交互中的本体感知信号为与同伴无关的意图感知协作搬运提供了可扩展的接口。
html_content:
PAINT: Partner-Agnostic Intent-Aware Cooperative Transport with Legged Robots
Zhihao Cao
1,∗
, Tianxu An
1,2,∗
, Chenhao Li
1,3
, Stelian Coros
2
, Marco Hutter
1
This work was supported by Hilti AG, Schaan, Liechtenstein, the Luxembourg National Research Fund (Ref. 18990533), and the Swiss National Science Foundation (Ref. 200021E_229503).
∗
These authors are contributed equally to this work
1
Robotic Systems Lab, ETH Zurich, Switzerland
2
Computational Robotics Lab, ETH Zurich, Switzerland
3
ETH AI Center, ETH Zurich, Switzerland
Abstract
Collaborative transport requires robots to infer partner intent through physical interaction while maintaining stable loco-manipulation. This becomes particularly challenging in complex environments, where interaction signals are difficult to capture and model. We present PAINT, a lightweight yet efficient hierarchical learning framework for partner-agonistic intent-aware collaborative legged transport that infers partner intent directly from proprioceptive feedback. PAINT decouples intent understanding from terrain-robust locomotion: A high-level policy infers the partner interaction wrench using an intent estimator and a teacher-student training scheme, while a low-level locomotion backbone ensures robust execution.
This enables lightweight deployment without external force-torque sensing or payload tracking. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate compliant cooperative transport across diverse terrains, payloads, and partners. Furthermore, we show that PAINT naturally scales to decentralized multi-robot transport and transfers across robot embodiments by swapping the underlying locomotion backbone. Our results suggest that proprioceptive signals in payload-coupled interaction provide a scalable interface for partner-agnostic intent-aware collaborative transport.
I
Introduction
Collaborative transport in heterogeneous teams of humans and robots is an important topic with applications in factory assistance and field operations. Collaborative robots can substantially reduce a partner’s physical workload by assisting in the transport of large and heavy payloads
[
11
]
.
In practical collaborative transport, one partner typically acts as the leader, initiating the desired motion of the payload, while the collaborator follows by interpreting the leader’s motion intent.
Effective collaboration requires follower robots to maintain robust, terrain-aware locomotion under load while simultaneously inferring the partner’s motion intent to achieve smooth, compliant transport
[
4
]
.
To be viable for real-world applications across diverse scenarios, the robot should be able to collaborate with heterogeneous partners, including humans and robots, across varying team sizes.
In this work, we aim to develop a control pipeline that enables a robot to interpret and follow a leader’s motion intent during collaborative transport. Motion intent is represented by the force and torque that the leader applies to the payload to guide the transport movement
[
24
,
2
,
17
]
. The leading partner may be either a human or another robot, and the transport team may include additional heterogeneous robots. Our goal is therefore to enable partner-agnostic collaboration during transport, inferring motion intent from the leading partner, while maintaining robust locomotion over uneven terrains. In addition, to minimize system overhead for practical applications, such as factories and construction sites, the pipeline must be lightweight and easily deployable.
However, achieving this goal remains challenging for several reasons. First, motion intent is hard to capture and model across diverse environments with heterogeneous human-robot teams. For this reason, many intent-aware transport systems rely on external sensors, such as
Force-Torque
(
FT
) sensors, to estimate interaction intent
[
24
,
2
,
17
]
. Although informative, such sensors are often too expensive and fragile for high-impact, high-wear deployment on factory floors, and are susceptible to locomotion-induced noise that is hard to filter out. Second, most existing intent-aware frameworks are validated only on flat, structured laboratory terrain
[
21
,
8
,
5
]
. Their robustness on uneven terrain remains insufficiently explored, where the robot must maintain stable locomotion while correctly responding to the partner’s intent.
Finally, most previous studies focus on single human-robot transport pairs, while extending such interaction mechanisms to larger teams remains less explored. Existing multi-robot transport systems often rely on motion capture infrastructure
[
25
]
, remote control
[
16
]
, or predefined team configurations
[
3
,
1
]
. Such assumptions limit portability to infrastructure-free deployment, particularly in outdoor or field settings, and make it harder to team with a flexible number of robots or heterogeneous partners.
To this end, we propose
PAINT
, a lightweight yet efficient hierarchical learning framework for
P
artner-
A
gnostic
INT
ent-aware cooperative transport using only accessible joint-level proprioceptive signals. While PAINT is robot-agnostic and applies to other morphologies, we specifically focus on quadrupedal robots for their robust locomotion on uneven terrains
[
14
,
6
]
.
PAINT decouples intent understanding from terrain-robust locomotion, where a
High-Level
(
HL
) transport policy infers partner intent from proprioceptive histories without
FT
sensing or payload tracking, and a
Low-Level
(
LL
) locomotion backbone tracks base motion commands and stabilizes the robot in diverse uneven terrains.
We adopt a teacher-student learning scheme to make the learned
HL
policy deployable under partial observability: a privileged teacher is trained with ground-truth interaction wrench data, while a student learns to reconstruct the interaction wrench via an intent estimator network from proprioception
[
18
,
27
]
. PAINT can be zero-shot deployed to collaborative transport systems with team-member-agnostic coordination interface. When multiple robots interact through a shared payload, their collective behavior is facilitated by payload-coupled signals. This allows the team-level intent-aware interface to extend naturally to decentralized multi-robot transport and to transfer across robot embodiments by swapping the locomotion backbone. The contributions of this work are as follows.
•
A lightweight yet efficient hierarchical learning framework.
We introduce a hierarchical framework that decouples partner-intent inference from terrain-robust locomotion, which simplifies policy learning across diverse environments. By leveraging a pretrained
LL
locomotion backbone, the
HL
policy can focus on generating transport behaviors aligned with partner motion intent, making the overall pipeline lightweight, efficient, and scalable.
•
Intent-aware
HL
policy.
We design an intent estimator and a teacher-student training pipeline for
HL
policy to infer partner motion intent purely from joint-level proprioceptive signals, enabling deployment with diverse partners and payloads without
FT
sensors at the end-effector or explicit payload tracking.
•
Scalability to decentralized multi-robot transport.
We show that the learned intent-aware formulation is not only partner-agnostic but can also be zero-shot transferred to multi-robot teams of flexible sizes. Through extensive simulation and real-world experiments, we provide evidence that pure proprioceptive signals from payload-coupled interaction provide a scalable interface for decentralized team transport.
Figure 1
:
Overview of the partner-agnostic intent-aware cooperative transport framework.
The system contains privileged training in simulation and lightweight real-world deployment via a hierarchical controller:
(A) Intent (Force/Torque) Generator:
In simulation, random forces and torques are applied to the payload to simulate partner guidance, producing diverse intent signals.
(B) High-Level Controller:
A HL policy maps intent to base commands and arm joint actions. The HL policy is trained with teacher-student scheme, where a teacher
(B1)
uses wrench observations, and a deployable student
(B2)
relies on proprioceptive histories with an intent estimator network to infer interaction wrench; KL regularization
(B3)
distills the teacher’s action distribution into the student.
(C) Low-Level Controller:
A terrain-robust locomotion backbone tracks the HL planar commands and outputs leg actions for stable loco-manipulation under load.
(D) Evaluation:
The framework is developed in simulation
(D1)
and transferred to real-world deployment
(D2)
, where it operates without end-effector
FT
sensing or payload tracking.
II
Related Work
Cooperative transport is a classical case of
Human-Robot Collaboration
(
HRC
). Early work emphasizes compliance via impedance-admittance regulation, enabling intuitive leader-follower behaviors and safe assistance
[
7
]
. Recent advancements are extending classical compliant cooperative transport toward intent-aware cooperative transport
[
24
,
2
,
17
,
21
]
. Existing intent-aware approaches broadly follow two directions. Model-based methods achieve robustness through constraint-aware reasoning and structured inference
[
24
,
21
,
8
,
5
,
22
]
. They are effective under reliable state observability, but depend on accurate modeling. In contrast, learning-based intent encoding can better tolerate contact variability, adapt to complex payload-coupled dynamics, and enable richer coordination behaviors
[
4
,
2
,
16
,
1
]
. However, several systems still rely on
FT
sensing
[
24
,
2
,
17
,
19
]
, which improves observability but increases deployment overhead due to cost and fragility. This has motivated lightweight alternatives that infer partner intent with only joint states and body dynamics
[
4
,
21
]
, without
FT
sensors at the end-effector. Beyond the sensing modality, intent representation also matters. Explicit representation
[
28
]
makes the inferred intent physically meaningful and improves human interpretability. It also makes the intent estimator easy to modularize for downstream control.
In addition, cooperative transport can build on progress in legged loco-manipulation. Legged systems are gaining attention because it naturally addresses rough terrain
[
4
,
22
]
. Whole-body frameworks
[
18
,
15
]
are widely adopted for coordinating the robot’s arm and legs. However, this end-to-end control framework prevents reuse of established locomotion backbones
[
14
,
6
,
10
]
across diverse terrains, payloads, and partners, thereby requiring costly and time-consuming retraining. Instead, hierarchical designs can offer a simpler and more practical integration pathway
[
12
,
28
]
.
Beyond single-robot
HRC
, multi-robot cooperative transport introduces additional sensing and coordination challenges. Prior work stabilizes collaboration via centralized tracking and planning
[
1
]
, motion capture
[
25
]
, remote control
[
16
]
, or fixed team configurations
[
3
,
1
,
23
]
, limiting scalability and field deployment.
In addition, limited attention has been paid to inferring the team-level intent from physical payload-coupled interactions during multi-robot cooperative transport
[
4
]
.
Our work targets an explicit intent representation learned from proprioceptive histories from payload-coupled physical interactions during collaborative transport, designed to pair with terrain-robust locomotion backbones and to naturally extend to team-level intent-aware coordination in multi-robot transport.
III
Method
III-A
Overview
We formulate partner-agnostic intent-aware cooperative transport under physical coupling as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP)

III-C
Intent Estimator in HL Student Policy
A key challenge in deployable intent-aware collaborative transport is that the interaction wrench at the payload is typically unobservable. Although
FT
sensors can measure interaction wrenches, they are not always available for high-impact, high-wear deployment in practice.
Instead, time-correlated joint proprioceptive signals are real-world accessible observations and can be used to infer partner-applied
FT
during payload-coupled interaction.
Therefore, we augment the student HL policy with an intent estimator network that estimates the interaction wrench from proprioceptive histories. The predicted wrench is then fed back into the policy as part of its observation. This estimator can be interpreted as learning an inverse observation kernel: while the observation kernel
Ω
\Omega
maps the latent state
s
t
s_{t}
to the observation
o
t
o_{t}
, the estimator infers latent intent variables from the observed proprioceptive signals induced by interaction.

III-D
Teacher-Student Training with KL Regularization
We adopt a teacher-student scheme in HL policy training to improve deployability and learn richer responses to partner intent. The key idea is to first learn a well-shaped intent-to-motion mapping with privileged intent information, and then distill it into a deployable policy that only relies on proprioceptive observations.

1
Privileged teacher (Fig.
1
-B1)

HL
teacher policy
π
HL
T

using PPO, with access to privileged intent observation
o
wrench
o^{\text{wrench}}
.
The teacher learns an admittance-like mapping from interaction wrenches to arm and base motion commands while remaining robust to load variations and terrain-induced disturbances introduced during training.

(a)
Cooperative transport across diverse terrains, payloads, and team configurations.
The quadruped-arm system reliably infers partner intent from arm proprioceptive histories without
FT
sensors, enabling stable co-carrying (I) across uneven terrains (e.g. stairs and gravel road), (II) with different payload (e.g. short and long box), and (III) with different partners (e.g. human and humanoid).
(b)
Cooperative transport with multi-robot teams and diverse payloads in simulation (I) and real world (II).
The proposed approach scales from pairwise co-carrying to decentralized teams of 2-4 legged robots that jointly move large, heavy, and irregular payloads.
(c)
Cooperative transport with diverse legged robots.
The proposed high-level intent policy enables robust co-carrying with a one-arm ANYmal (I) over rough or stepping terrain, and also generalizes to an ANYmal without an arm (II) for payload transport.

III-F

The objective of cooperative transport is to follow the partner’s intended motion while maintaining stable payload handling. As the interaction wrench applied by the partner indicates the intended motion, we design task rewards that align the robot base motion with the interaction wrench on the payload.

IV
Implementation Details
We train our policies in Isaac Gym using 4096 parallel environments on a single RTX 3080Ti GPU. We additionally randomize the payload mass to avoid overfitting to a single payload-dynamics regime, sampling the mass uniformly from
0
kg
0\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
to
10
kg
10\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
. The LL policy follows the setup in
[
12
]
. For the HL policy, both the teacher and the student use three-layer MLP actor and critic networks with hidden dimensions (128, 128, 128). The intent estimator network adopts the same MLP architecture. The teacher and student HL policies are trained for 15k and 20k iterations. To further increase policy robustness and reduce sim-to-real gaps, domain randomization is applied to the robot’s proprioceptive observations and the generated interactive wrench.
V
Experiments and Results
V-A
Real-World Transport Across Diverse Situations
Fig.
2(a)
demonstrates that the robot can reliably infer partner intent using only arm proprioceptive histories, without requiring
FT
sensors or payload tracking. Across uneven terrains, such as stairs and gravel, the learned HL policy produces smooth planar base commands that the
LL
locomotion backbone tracks robustly, resulting in stable co-carrying despite terrain-induced perturbations. Crucially, the method remains effective across variations in payload geometries and weights, indicating that the inferred intent is not overfitted to a single payload configuration. Finally, cooperative transport remains stable when partnering with different leaders, such as human and humanoid, supporting the claim that the proposed pipeline is partner-agnostic.
V-B
Quantitative Studies Across Different Payload Masses
Table
II
reports the single-agent quantitative results under different payload masses and the same interaction force and torque in simulation.
The compared baselines include Damped Arm, Pure RL-4
[
20
]
, BC Distillation-4
[
4
]
, and Force Estimator-4
[
18
,
27
]
. Damped Arm is implemented via classical PD control: the arm is in damping mode, and the end-effector displacement induced by external forces and torques is passed to the PD controller to compute base motion commands. A low-pass filter is used to improve motion smoothness. All other methods in baseline comparisons use 4-step (
0.08
s
0.08\text{\,}\mathrm{s}
) proprioception histories, while in ablation studies, different history steps are compared.
As payload mass increases, all methods generally degrade, suggesting that heavier loads induce more challenging coupled dynamics. Across masses, PAINT remains the most robust method, consistently achieving the best or near-best metrics. Notably, although Force Est.-4 achieves competitive
FT
estimation accuracy, its tracking errors are typically higher than those of PAINT-4, suggesting that PAINT benefits not only from informative interaction wrenches but also from the teacher-student distillation. The end-effector metrics further support this trend, with PAINT generally producing lower constraint
FT
and stronger intent alignment than the baselines. In ablation studies, we show that temporal history is critical under partial observability, where reducing history steps consistently degrades both tracking and interaction performance, whereas longer histories further improve robustness.
V-C
Scaling to Multi-Robot Teams
Fig.
2(b)
evaluates multi-robot scalability by deploying the same transport policy on teams of 2-4 legged robots transporting large and heavy payloads in simulation and the real world. The multi-robot teams successfully transport various payloads, including a sofa, a desk, and a barrel, over uneven terrain while maintaining coherent motions in simulation, and jointly carry an
8
kg
8\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
long box with a human leader in the real world. Fig.
4
shows the improved performance of multi-robot teams when transporting heavy payloads. In the multi-robot setting, PAINT maintains high intent alignment across payloads up to
28
kg
28\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
.
Although trained in a single-agent setting with up to
10
kg
10\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
payload, PAINT remains effective under challenging payload-coupled dynamics with higher payload masses and team sizes unseen from training. Notably, this coordination remains fully decentralized, with each robot running the same policy and inferring the team-level intent solely from its individual proprioceptive histories, without centralized planning or explicit state sharing.
Figure 3
:
Saliency analysis of the intent estimator under a sequential interaction schedule across arm joint positions.
Figure 4
:
End-effector intent-alignment across payload masses. Means (boxes) and standard deviations (lines) are computed over 4096 environments, comparing single-agent and multi-agent transport from
12
kg

to

kg
28\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
payloads.
V-D
Scaling to Different Robot Embodiments
In this experiment, we transfer PAINT to ANYmal without an arm. For the new robot embodiment, the
HL
policy takes proprioceptive signals from leg joints as input and outputs base motion commands for its
LL
locomotion backbone. Fig.
2(c)
shows that both ANYmal with an arm and without arm achieve robust cooperative transport in simulation and in real-world trials over rough and stepping terrain. This indicates that the intent estimator can infer interaction wrenches from proprioception of either arm or leg joints, as both body parts can encode meaningful interaction cues. In Fig.
5
, we show how the two robot embodiments track a synthetic yet human-like interaction wrench profile. The synthetic wrench command is generated by the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process to model realistic human-induced
FT
[
13
]
. The measured wrench data closely track the command profile with smooth responses, indicating that the PAINT pipeline can not only scale to different robot embodiments, but also facilitate smooth and compliant collaboration with unseen partner interaction profiles. The bottom row of Fig.
2(b)
further demonstrates heterogeneous multi-robot transport in the real world, where two different robot embodiments jointly carry an
8
kg
8\text{\,}\mathrm{kg}
long box with a human leader.
Figure 5
:
Measured and commanded interaction wrenches on the payload for two robot embodiments in simulation.
V-E
Saliency Analysis of Intent Estimation
To better understand how partner intent is estimated from proprioceptive signals, we perform a gradient-based saliency analysis to reveal how the intent estimator dynamically shifts its attention across individual arm joints under varying interaction modes. Specifically, we compute the gradient of the scalar mean of the estimated interaction cue
β
t
est
\mathbf{\beta}^{\text{est}}_{t}
with respect to the transport proprioception
o
t
trans
o^{\text{trans}}_{t}
. A higher gradient magnitude indicates that the estimator is more sensitive to a particular joint channel, as instantaneous fluctuations in that channel more profoundly influence the output. As illustrated in Fig.
3
, the attention patterns exhibit distinct spatial and temporal shifts depending on the applied force or torque on the payload. For clarity, we focus on joint position inputs from
o
t
trans
o^{\text{trans}}_{t}
, as velocity and action inputs demonstrated redundant, less salient patterns. When a step force is applied along the y-direction, saliency is primarily localized at the shoulder rotation joint. In contrast, estimated interaction cues along the x-direction are predominantly derived from the flexion joints of the shoulder, elbow, and wrist. Estimating yaw interaction appears to require more complex and combinatorial signaling, though the most salient response originates from the forearm rotation joint. Furthermore, we observe a short and consistent temporal lag between the onset of external interaction and the emergence of salient responses. This latency suggests that the estimator undergoes an integration period to accumulate the joint-evolution patterns before forming a stable, informative intent representation. This result supports the use of proprioceptive histories to overcome partial observability during collaborative transport tasks. Combined with the results in Section
V-C
, the saliency analysis also provides insight into the successful scaling of a single-robot policy to multi-robot teams: by leveraging distinct attention patterns on the proprioceptive signals from payload-coupled interaction, each robot can infer team-level interaction cues locally, enabling decentralized cooperative transport without centralized planning or explicit state sharing.
Figure 6
:
The
HL
policy reacts purely to proprioceptive signals without active obstacle avoidance. When approaching obstacles (left), it cannot proactively adjust its motion, leading to collisions (middle) and causing locomotion failure (right).
V-F
Failure Cases and Limitations
The proposed framework has limitations in environments requiring active obstacle avoidance. As shown in Fig.
6
, when encountering obstacles such as walls or narrow structures, the
HL
policy lacks explicit perception or planning capabilities to proactively avoid collisions. Instead, it passively follows the leader’s motion, sending unsafe base commands to the
LL
locomotion backbone, which can cause locomotion failure when the leading partner does not provide sufficient corrective guidance.
VI
Conclusion and Future Work
We present PAINT, a lightweight yet efficient partner-agnostic intent-aware framework for cooperative transport with legged robots. By combining a terrain-robust
LL
locomotion backbone with a
HL
transport policy that infers partner intent purely from joint-level proprioceptive histories, our approach enables lightweight deployment without
FT
sensors or payload tracking. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework supports stable cooperative transport across diverse environments, and generalizes across different payloads, team compositions, and robot embodiments. Furthermore, we validate that proprioceptive signals induced by payload-coupled interactions can encode meaningful interaction cues, overcoming partial observability in both robot-leader and multi-robot transport teams.
Several directions remain for future work. First, partner intent is represented by a planar wrench, which is not sufficient for more complex environments. Full 6D interaction wrenches and more semantic-aware intent representations can be explored. Second, the current
HL
policy allows only limited arm motion. Future research can enable the arm to move more freely, thus improving the ability to perform payload reorientation and handle constrained maneuvers, such as turning in narrow corridors. Lastly, collaborative grasping and lifting can be incorporated to make the pipeline more complete and practical for payload transportation.
VII

preview_text:
Collaborative transport requires robots to infer partner intent through physical interaction while maintaining stable loco-manipulation. This becomes particularly challenging in complex environments, where interaction signals are difficult to capture and model. We present PAINT, a lightweight yet efficient hierarchical learning framework for partner-agonistic intent-aware collaborative legged transport that infers partner intent directly from proprioceptive feedback. PAINT decouples intent understanding from terrain-robust locomotion: A high-level policy infers the partner interaction wrench using an intent estimator and a teacher-student training scheme, while a low-level locomotion backbone ensures robust execution. This enables lightweight deployment without external force-torque sensing or payload tracking. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate compliant cooperative transport across diverse terrains, payloads, and partners. Furthermore, we show that PAINT naturally scales to decentralized multi-robot transport and transfers across robot embodiments by swapping the underlying locomotion backbone. Our results suggest that proprioceptive signals in payload-coupled interaction provide a scalable interface for partner-agnostic intent-aware collaborative transport.

PAINT: Partner-Agnostic Intent-Aware Cooperative Transport with Legged Robots
Zhihao Cao
1,∗
, Tianxu An
1,2,∗
, Chenhao Li
1,3
, Stelian Coros
2
, Marco Hutter
1
This work was supported by Hilti AG, Schaan, Liechtenstein, the Luxembourg National Research Fund (Ref. 18990533), and the Swiss National Science Foundation (Ref. 200021E_229503).
∗
These authors are contributed equally to this work
